verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: With such a man the slanderer thought that I had been on terms of friendship, or rather he did not think so, but like an enemy invented an incredible fiction: for he knows full well that he has lied.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: I would that, whoever he is, he were present here, that I might put the question to him on the word of Truth itself (for whatever we speak as in the presence of God, we Christians consider as an oath); I say, that I might ask him this question, which of us rejoiced most in the well-being of the departed Constans? who prayed for him most earnestly?

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: The facts of the foregoing charge prove this; indeed it is plain to every one how the case stands.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: But although he himself knows full well, that no one who was so disposed towards the departed Constans, and who truly loved him, could be a friend to his enemy, I fear that being possessed with other feelings towards him than I was, he has falsely attributed to me those sentiments of hatred which were entertained by himself.
